flashcards.back: If n items are placed into m containers and n > m, then at least one container must contain more than one item. Named after Peter Gustav Lejeune Dirichlet (1834).

flashcards.front: What is the Generalized Pigeonhole Principle?
flashcards.back: If n items are placed into m containers, then at least one container contains at least ⌈n/m⌉ items. This is also called the Dirichlet box principle.

flashcards.front: What is the Strong Pigeonhole Principle?
flashcards.back: If n items are colored with k colors, then there exists a color with at least ⌈n/k⌉ items of that color. It's an extension to handle multisets and partitions.

flashcards.front: How does the Pigeonhole Principle apply to hashing?
flashcards.back: In hash tables, if there are n slots and we try to store n+1 items, collision is guaranteed (by pigeonhole principle). This is why load factor management is crucial.

flashcards.front: What is the Birthday Paradox in context of pigeonhole principle?
flashcards.back: With 23 people, there are 253 pairs but only 365 days. Since 253 > 365/2, probability of shared birthday exceeds 50%. This demonstrates the counterintuitive power of the principle.

flashcards.front: How do you prove there are two people in Delhi with same number of hairs?
flashcards.back: Human head has max ~150,000 hairs. Delhi has ~20 million people. Since 20,000,000 > 150,000, by pigeonhole principle, at least two people must have the same number of hairs.

flashcards.front: What is the formula for minimum number to guarantee repetition?
flashcards.back: If you have m categories, to guarantee at least one category has k items, you need (k-1)m + 1 items. Formula: (k-1)m + 1 = km - m + 1.

flashcards.front: What is Ramsey Theory's connection to pigeonhole principle?
flashcards.back: Ramsey numbers extend pigeonhole principle to graph coloring problems. R(m,n) is the smallest number of vertices to guarantee either an m-clique or n-independent set.

flashcards.front: How does Erdos-Szekeres theorem relate to pigeonhole principle?
flashcards.back: Any sequence of (r-1)(s-1)+1 distinct numbers contains either an increasing subsequence of length r or a decreasing subsequence of length s.

flashcards.front: What is the Pigeonhole Principle application in networking?
flashcards.back: In the TCP/IP model, if n packets are sent through m routers, at least one router must handle ⌈n/m⌉ packets. Also in frequency assignment to avoid interference.

flashcards.front: How is pigeonhole principle used in proving lower bounds in algorithms?
flashcards.back: If sorting algorithm uses only comparison outcomes as decision nodes and has n! possible results, it needs at least log₂(n!) = Ω(n log n) comparisons in worst case.
For any real number α and positive integer n, there exist integers p,q with 1 ≤ q ≤ n such that |α - p/q| < 1/(n+1). This is a number theory application.

flashcards.front: What is the inclusion-exclusion principle connection?
flashcards.back: Pigeonhole principle is a special case where we're counting minimum overlaps. If n items are placed in m sets, at least one set has ⌈n/m⌉ items.

flashcards.front: How does pigeonhole principle apply to handshaking?
flashcards.back: In any party of n people, if each person shakes hands with at least (n-1)/2 others, then there exist two people who shook hands with the same number of people.

flashcards.front: What is a combinatorial proof using pigeonhole principle?
flashcards.back: To prove: In any group of 6 people, either 3 mutually know each other or 3 mutually don't know each other. This is Ramsey number R(3,3) = 6.
